summary: Gruber responds to Ben Bajarin's suggestion that Apple's rumored headset deserves its own event rather than sharing the packed WWDC keynote. He argues the headset will indeed be announced at WWDC because there's too much evidence pointing to it, and Apple needs developers to start building for the platform immediately. He acknowledges the keynote will be unusually dense, covering iOS 17, macOS 14, WatchOS 10, new Mac hardware, and the headset with xrOS. His key argument is that Apple's post-COVID pre-recorded keynote format solves the time problem through tight editing, allowing them to cover far more ground than the old live on-stage format permitted.
key_points: WWDC keynotes are always packed with software updates across every platform, and this year adds potential Mac hardware and the headset
Apple needs to announce the headset at WWDC specifically because developers need to start building software for xrOS immediately
The pre-recorded 'keynote movie' format allows Apple to cover far more content in the same runtime than the old live on-stage presentations
The worst WWDC keynote (2015, with Jimmy Iovine and Apple Music) ran 2h:25m and felt under-rehearsed — Apple learned from that
Many iOS and macOS features will likely be cut from the keynote to make room, relegated to the afternoon State of the Union session
money_quotes: There's just too much smoke for there not to be a fire.
If they want developers to start creating software for the platform, this is the time.
Probably the worst WWDC keynote ever, and the only one that ever felt under-rehearsed.
Post-COVID keynotes aren't just pre-recorded, they're very tightly edited.
Fitting the headset and xrOS developer frameworks into the old-style on-stage WWDC keynote would have posed a problem. I don't think it's a problem with the new 'keynote movie' format.
We won't come out of the keynote thinking it was too long; we'll come out of it with our heads spinning because it's going to cover so much, so fast.
key_insight: Apple's shift to tightly edited pre-recorded keynotes solves the logistical problem of fitting a major new product category announcement into an already-packed WWDC, making a dedicated event unnecessary.
